nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich shallow coastal reefs, typically at depths of around 2-16 m; adults are sometimes seen in small aggregations

diet.notes: In the wild this species has been documented to feed almost exclusively on coral polyps (corallivore), with FishBase recording an obligate coral-polyp diet. Captive specimens are notoriously difficult to wean onto prepared foods and have a poor track record of long-term survival on aquarium diets. When acclimation is attempted, it is generally advised to offer a varied mix of live and frozen meaty preparations (mysis, enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped clam/shrimp), Aiptasia, and herbivore preparations multiple times per day, ideally introduced into a mature system with abundant live rock for grazing.

avoidWith: live corals (will nip and consume polyps)
other butterflyfish unless a bonded pair

summary: A striking small Western Indian Ocean butterflyfish with a yellow-orange body, dark facial bars, and jet-black dorsal, anal, and caudal fins. Generally considered an expert-only species due to its specialized corallivorous diet and poor track record in captivity.
careNotes:
Chaetodon melapterus is endemic to the Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, southern Red Sea, and Gulf of Aden, where it tends to inhabit coral-rich shallow reefs at depths of around 2-16 m. In the wild it has been documented as an obligate corallivore that feeds almost exclusively on hard- and soft-coral polyps, which makes it one of the more difficult butterflyfishes to maintain long-term in captivity. FishBase and specialist sources (Reeflex) describe its captive survival prospects as very poor without an extremely well-established system and skilled feeding regime.

Because of the obligate coral-polyp diet, this species is not reef safe — it has been known to consume LPS and soft coral polyps, fan worms, and may pick at Tridacna clams. It is best housed in a mature fish-only or FOWLR system with abundant live rock, plentiful grazing surfaces, and ample open swimming space. Aquarists report that some specimens will eventually accept enriched brine shrimp, mysis, finely chopped seafood, Aiptasia, and algae-based preparations, but many refuse prepared foods entirely, so a target-feeding routine and acclimation tank are commonly advised.

Like most butterflyfishes, this species tends to form monogamous pairs in the wild; in aquariums it is generally advised to keep a single specimen or an established mated pair, with simultaneous introduction of any pair.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: The flagged tank-size source disagreement has been investigated with two additional sources (Wikipedia, Violet Aquarium) plus re-verification of LiveAquaria and Quality Marine. The full source spread is now: LiveAquaria 55 gal (208 L), Reef2Reef 55 gal (208 L), Violet Aquarium 60 gal (~227 L), Quality Marine 75 gal (284 L), Ultimate Reef 101-250 L, and Reeflex 1500 L (specialist long-term figure). The retail/hobbyist mainstream consensus clusters at 55-75 gal (208-284 L) for a fish that reaches only 13 cm TL. The outlier Reeflex 1500 L figure appears to reflect an aspirational long-term specialist recommendation rather than a true minimum. The chosen value of 400 L is retained as a deliberate editorial decision: it sits above the retail floor (giving this expert-only, obligate corallivore adequate grazing area and swimming room) without endorsing the extreme specialist figure, and is the next step up from 300 L in the approved nice-values list. Adult size consensus confirmed across all sources at 13 cm / 5 in. Diet characterisation retained as obligate corallivore per FishBase and Reeflex; LiveAquaria's more optimistic omnivore framing is noted but the cautious expert-level view is considered more appropriate for this profile.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to form monogamous pairs in the wild that spawn via pelagic eggs (oviparous). Captive breeding is generally considered not achieved at any meaningful scale for this species, as for most butterflyfishes — the pelagic larvae are very difficult to rear and the adults are challenging just to maintain in captivity.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care/husbandry profile: 55 gal min tank, 5 in max size, peaceful temperament, expert-only care, not reef safe, omnivore including Aiptasia and meaty/algae foods, 75-82°F, pH 8.0-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used in place of the designated Reef2Reef thread (which returned HTTP 403). Provided 13 cm TL max size, wild temperature 25.2-29.3°C (mean 27.1°C), distribution (Western Indian Ocean: Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, southern Red Sea of Yemen), reef-associated/non-migratory habitat, taxonomy (Chaetodontidae, Acanthuriformes), and importantly the obligate corallivore feeding ecology.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Specialist hobby encyclopedia. Cross-checked size (13 cm), temperature (25-28°C), depth (2-16 m), expanded country list, and recommended minimum tank of 1500 L (used as the upper end of the source spread for the tank-size decision). Reinforced the FishBase view that this is a near-obligate coral-polyp feeder with very poor captive survival.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Wholesale care profile. Cross-checked min tank size (75 gal / 284 L), 5 in adult size, temperature 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, dKH 8-12, SG 1.020-1.025, and the consensus that the species is not reef safe and may pick at corals and crustaceans.

fish.breedingNotes: Forms monogamous pairs in the wild; oviparous with pelagic larvae. Captive rearing of the pelagic-larval stage has not been meaningfully achieved for this species.
